metadata.seed: Things? You know, I've been really sort of preoccupied and anxious a lot this weekend. My daughter got sick, so I had to take three days off of work to take care of her. And I'm starting to worry. She's been sick a lot since I've gone back to work, and she's in daycare, and I've had to take a lot of time off work, and I'm getting close to going over my allotted amount of days. And so I'm finding myself being very anxious about that and then feeling guilty because when I'm trying to take care of her, I'm always worrying about work. So.

patient.query: Things? You know, I've been really sort of preoccupied and anxious a lot this weekend. My daughter got sick, so I had to take three days off of work to take care of her. And I'm starting to worry. She's been sick a lot since I've gone back to work, and she's in daycare, and I've had to take a lot of time off work, and I'm getting close to going over my allotted amount of days. And so I'm finding myself being very anxious about that and then feeling guilty because when I'm trying to take care of her, I'm always worrying about work. So.

automatic_thoughts: I'm worried about my daughter's health
I'm going to run out of sick days
I feel guilty for worrying about work

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot right now, juggling your daughter's health and your work responsibilities. What thoughts are going through your mind when you find yourself worrying about work while you're trying to care for her?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot right now – juggling your daughter’s health and your work responsibilities, and feeling anxious and guilty in the process. That’s a really tough spot to be in.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot right now, juggling your daughter's health and your work responsibilities. What thoughts are going through your mind when you find yourself worrying about work while you're trying to care for her?

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot right now, juggling your daughter's health and your work responsibilities. What’s going through your mind when you imagine what a supportive friend or colleague might say about your situation?

safety_check.response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot right now, juggling your daughter's health and work responsibilities. When you say you're feeling anxious, are you having any thoughts of wanting to not be here?

patient.query: It's just... overwhelming, you know? I keep thinking that if I don’t handle things at work perfectly, I’ll get in trouble or let people down. But then, when I’m with my daughter, I feel guilty for not being fully present, like my mind is always somewhere else. It’s like I can’t escape this pressure.

automatic_thoughts: if I don’t handle things at work perfectly, I’ll get in trouble

entailment: Worried about being a bad mother (finding)
Anxiety about not coping with parenthood (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly difficult to be juggling those demands and feeling pulled in so many directions. Can you tell me more about what "perfectly" looks like at work?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds incredibly difficult to be juggling so much and feeling pulled in different directions. It makes sense that you'd feel overwhelmed when you're carrying those kinds of thoughts and worries.

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel hard. I guess “perfectly” means being on top of every project, never making mistakes, and always having the right answers. I see my colleagues managing things smoothly, and I can’t help but think that I should be like them. But then I just feel so inadequate and anxious, like I'm just waiting to mess up.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you've identified a really clear picture of what "perfect" looks like, and how that's impacting how you feel when you compare yourself to your colleagues – what makes you think that being like them is necessary?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like you're putting a lot of pressure on yourself to be "perfect," and it's understandably tough when you compare yourself to your colleagues. It makes sense that you'd feel inadequate and anxious when you're holding yourself to such a high standard.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds like you've identified a really clear picture of what "perfect" looks like, and how that's impacting how you feel when you compare yourself to your colleagues – what makes you think that being like them is necessary?

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds like you're holding yourself to a really high standard, and it's tough when you feel like you're not meeting it. What do you think a supportive friend might say about your expectations of yourself?

safety_check.response: It sounds like you're carrying a lot right now, and it's tough feeling that pressure. When you say you're waiting to mess up, were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: I don't know... maybe because they seem so confident and put-together? It just feels like if I can’t meet those standards, I won’t be respected or taken seriously. I guess it’s scary to think about being seen as less capable, especially when I want to set a good example for my daughter. It's like I’m constantly in a battle with myself.

triggers: wanting to set a good example

entailment: Worried about being a bad father (finding)
Worried about being a bad mother (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like seeing others who appear confident triggers thoughts about your own capabilities and worthiness – what makes you believe that not meeting those standards would mean you wouldn't be respected?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like seeing others who appear confident triggers a lot of worry and self-doubt for you, and it’s tough to feel like you’re constantly battling yourself. It makes sense that you'd want to set a good example for your daughter, and it's understandable to feel anxious when you're concerned about not meeting those standards.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds like seeing others who appear confident triggers thoughts about your own capabilities and worthiness – what makes you believe that not meeting those standards would mean you wouldn't be respected?

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds like seeing others who appear confident triggers a lot of worry for you – and it's understandable to want to set a good example for your daughter. What do you think your daughter might say about your efforts, even if you don’t feel you’re meeting those standards?

safety_check.response: It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of internal conflict and worry when you compare yourself to others, and that's understandably distressing. When you mentioned battling with yourself, were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: I guess it’s just how I’ve always seen things. I grew up hearing that you have to achieve to be valued, you know? And if I can’t keep up, I feel like people will think I'm not good enough or that I don’t deserve to be there. I just don’t want my daughter to see me that way, like I’m failing in any sense. It’s like I’m constantly afraid of being judged.

triggers: hearing that you have to achieve to be valued
automatic_thoughts: people will think I'm not good enough
I don’t deserve to be there

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
Worried about being a bad mother (finding)
Worried about being a bad father (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like that message about achievement being tied to value had a powerful impact on you, and it’s understandable that you’re feeling anxious about how your daughter perceives you. Can you tell me more about what specifically makes you think people might judge you?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like you've developed a really strong belief system around achievement and worth, and it's understandably creating a lot of anxiety and fear of judgment. It takes courage to recognize how deeply ingrained these patterns are, and I appreciate you sharing that with me.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds like that message about achievement being tied to value had a powerful impact on you, and it’s understandable that you’re feeling anxious about how your daughter perceives you. Can you tell me more about what specifically makes you think people might judge you?

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds like that message about achievement being tied to your worth has had a powerful impact on how you see yourself and your interactions with your daughter. What do you think your daughter might say if she saw you struggling and worried about being judged?

safety_check.response: It sounds incredibly difficult to carry that belief that you need to achieve to be valued, and it's understandable that you'd feel anxious and afraid of judgment. When you say you feel like you’re failing, are you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: Yeah, I think it’s just little things, really. Like when someone asks for help and I stumble over my words or don’t have an answer right away, it feels like I’m letting everyone down. Or when I see others sharing their accomplishments, and I feel like I just fade into the background. It’s so hard not to feel that critical gaze, even when I know it’s probably all in my head.

neutral: Ability to understand own emotions (observable entity)
Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like these situations are bringing up some really difficult feelings – that sense of letting people down and feeling like you're being judged. Can you tell me more about what goes through your mind when you notice yourself stumbling over your words?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like these situations are bringing up a lot of difficult feelings, and it takes courage to share that. It's understandable that you'd feel that way when you notice those things happening.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds like these situations are bringing up some really difficult feelings – that sense of letting people down and feeling like you're being judged. Can you tell me more about what goes through your mind when you notice yourself stumbling over your words?

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds like these situations are bringing up a lot of difficult feelings for you. Imagine a friend was experiencing this – what do you think they might be telling themselves about the situation?

safety_check.response: That sounds really difficult, and it takes courage to share that. When you mentioned feeling like you're letting people down, were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: When that happens, my heart races, and I just feel so embarrassed. I start thinking that everyone sees me as incompetent or that they’re laughing at me inside. It’s like this wave of shame washes over me, and I just want to disappear. I really hate how quickly I can turn a small moment into something so big in my head.

automatic_thoughts: I can turn a small moment into something big

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response:
Okay, it sounds incredibly distressing to experience that racing heart, embarrassment, and that overwhelming feeling of wanting to disappear. It takes a lot of courage to share something so vulnerable. It's understandable that you'd feel that way when you're experiencing those thoughts about what others might be thinking of you.

It sounds like you're noticing a pattern where a situation triggers thoughts about being seen as incompetent or being laughed at, which then leads to feelings of shame and a strong desire to avoid the situation. You've also identified that you tend to amplify these moments in your mind.

Could you tell me a little more about what makes those thoughts – "everyone sees me as incompetent" or "they're laughing at me inside" – feel so true in those moments?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that racing heart, embarrassment, and shame, especially when you feel like a small moment escalates into something much larger in your mind. It takes a lot of courage to share that, and I appreciate you describing what's happening for you.

socratic_questioning.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that wave of shame and the urge to disappear. Can you tell me more about what specifically makes you think everyone sees you as incompetent in those moments?

alternative_perspective.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that wave of shame and want to disappear. If a friend were watching this unfold, what do you think they might notice about the situation, or how they might interpret what's happening?

safety_check.response: That sounds incredibly distressing, and it makes sense you'd want to disappear when you're feeling that overwhelmed. When you said that, were you having any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be here?

patient.query: It just feels so real, like I can almost hear their judgments, even if nobody says anything out loud. Sometimes I think back to things that happened in the past, and it’s like those moments replay in my mind, feeding the fear that I’ll mess up again. I guess I’ve built up this idea that I have to be perfect, or else people won’t respect me, and when I stumble, it feels like proof that I’m failing. It’s a spiral, and I keep

automatic_thoughts: I have to be perfect, or else people won’t respect me
When I stumble, it feels like proof that I’m failing

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
neutral: Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to feel those judgments so intensely, and to have those past experiences keep resurfacing. Can you tell me more about what makes you feel like those past moments are so relevant to what's happening now?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds incredibly distressing to feel like you're constantly replaying past moments and anticipating judgment, and it makes sense that you'd feel like you're failing when you stumble. That feeling of being in a spiral can be really overwhelming.

patient.query: It’s like… every little mistake from before feels like a marker of who I am now. I think about times when I didn’t say the right thing or when I felt embarrassed, and they just linger. It’s terrifying to think those moments define me, and it’s hard to shake that feeling when I'm in similar situations now. I guess I worry that if I fail again, it’ll just confirm those fears I already have about myself.

triggers: times when I didn’t say the right thing
automatic_thoughts: if I fail again, it’ll confirm my fears

entailment: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

It feels like every little failure adds to this identity I have that says I'm just not good at anything. I guess I’m always worried people will see those mistakes and not see the person I want to be.

automatic_thoughts: I'm just not good at anything
